---
title: "Many samples at once: the param_list"
description: "Process a whole cohort in one run, with per-sample and shared arguments."
order: 65
---

Real studies have many samples, and most arguments are the same for all of them
while a few differ per sample. The **`param_list`** captures exactly that shape:
it is a list where **each entry is one sample**, carrying that sample's own
arguments, while any argument set **outside** the list applies to **every**
sample.

```yaml
param_list:
  - id: sample_1
    input: data/sample_1.h5mu
    rna_min_counts: 2          # per-sample override
  - id: sample_2
    input: data/sample_2.h5mu
    rna_min_counts: 1          # per-sample override
rna_max_counts: 1000000        # shared: applied to all samples
```

## Why it matters

- **Global vs. per-sample is explicit.** Shared defaults live in one place;
  overrides sit next to the sample they affect. Nothing is repeated, so nothing
  drifts out of sync.
- **One command, many samples.** No shell loops, no re-running the pipeline once
  per sample.
- **Parallelism for free.** Nextflow treats each entry as its own job and runs
  them concurrently, on whatever compute the [run is on](../get-started/index.qmd).

## Two ways to write it

The `param_list` is the same idea whether you pass it as YAML inside a
`-params-file`, as shown above, or as a **CSV** with one row per sample and one
column per argument (`--param_list samples.csv`). The CSV form is handy for large
cohorts; see [Ingest 10x Genomics data](../guides/ingest-10x.qmd#ingest-many-samples-at-once)
for an example.

See a `param_list` in a full run in the [Get started](../get-started/index.qmd)
tutorial, and [Process many samples](../guides/process-many-samples.qmd) for the
task recipe.
